title: The First Sunday of Advent
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
Sermon Date: November 29, 2020

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the First Sunday of Advent, marking the beginning of the Christian liturgical year in the Western church calendar. The Revised Common Lectionary readings would have included Isaiah 64:1-9, Psalm 80:1-7, 16-18, 1 Corinthians 1:3-9, and Mark 13:24-37, all emphasizing themes of divine judgment, restoration, and watchful waiting. Advent is a season of four Sundays preceding Christmas, traditionally focused on preparation, repentance, and anticipation of Christ's coming. The color for the season is purple/violet, reflecting both penitence and royalty.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the early stages of recovery and transition following General Convention 2018, with ongoing debates about sexuality and inclusion that had shaped the denomination. The Diocese of Colorado was led by Bishop Kimberly Lucas (elected 2018), who was focusing on pandemic-era ministry adaptations as COVID-19 significantly impacted in-person worship across the diocese. Saint John's Cathedral itself would have been navigating hybrid worship models—some in-person attendance with strict protocols alongside livestream options, a reality that shaped all Episcopal parishes in November 2020. No major denominational crises or elections were occurring at this specific moment.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Colorado was entering a critical surge phase of COVID-19, with Denver hospitals approaching capacity and the state implementing new restrictions just days before this sermon; this would have weighed heavily on any congregation gathering in person. The Colorado legislature had recently completed its session, and statewide attention was focused on the post-election period following the November 3 general election, with election certification underway. Weather in late November in Denver is typically cold but dry, with the city having experienced an early season snow in October. The Capitol Hill neighborhood, where Saint John's Cathedral is located, has a significant arts and LGBTQ+ community that would have been affected by pandemic isolation.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The United States was four weeks past the November 3 presidential election, with the race between incumbent President Donald Trump and President-elect Joe Biden still in legal challenge phases; Trump was refusing to concede and pursuing litigation in multiple states. The COVID-19 pandemic was reaching record daily case numbers across the nation, with the U.S. regularly exceeding 100,000 new cases per day and hospitals in urban centers becoming overwhelmed. The Thanksgiving holiday had just passed (November 26, 2020), and public health officials were expressing grave concern about transmission spikes resulting from holiday gatherings, which would shape the sermon's potential emphasis on "watching" and preparation.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The United Kingdom was in its second national COVID-19 lockdown, with strict restrictions affecting worship and community life in the Church of England, creating a shared Anglican experience of pandemic constraint. The Iranian government was dealing with the aftermath of the November 27 assassination of nuclear scientist Mohsen Fakhrizadeh, which escalated tensions in the Middle East and international diplomatic circles. The global COVID-19 death toll had recently surpassed 1.3 million, with particular crises unfolding in Europe and India as winter approached in the Northern Hemisphere.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The national mood was deeply fractious and anxious—the unresolved election aftermath combined with surging COVID-19 cases and the approaching holidays created a sense of uncertainty and dread rather than traditional holiday joy. Religious institutions like Saint John's Cathedral were experiencing profound tension between theological calling (gathering community) and practical responsibility (preventing disease transmission), a tension that would have made Advent's theme of "watchful waiting" feel particularly urgent and relevant. Entertainment and sports provided some cultural continuity—the NFL season was proceeding with protocols, and holiday entertainment was shifting to streaming platforms—but the general zeitgeist was one of isolation, division, and anticipatory anxiety rather than celebration.
